text: The video demonstrates a robotically-assisted total pancreatectomy with autologous islet transplantation. Laparoscopic instruments are used to mobilize the right colon down to the terminal ileum. The transverse duodenum is mobilized from the ligament of Treitz using a Cattell maneuver, and an opening is made into the foramen of Winslow. The jejunum is pulled into the right upper quadrant and transected with a 3.5mm linear cutting stapler approximately 10cm from the ligament of Treitz. The inferior pancreatic border is dissected towards the tail, visible at the top of the screen, and the splenic flexure is taken down.

The da Vinci robot is docked at the head of the table. The surgeon uses hook cautery to divide the tissue overlying the porta hepatis, exposing the superior border of the pancreas and the common hepatic artery. The splenic artery is isolated with a vessel loop. The gastroduodenal artery is cleared of surrounding tissue and isolated with a vessel loop. The gastric artery is identified, clipped twice proximally and once distally, and divided between the clips.

The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the duodenum and divides the duodenum using a 3.8 mm linear cutting stapler. The retropancreatic space is developed, with the camera angle providing a view from the caudal to cephalad direction into the retropancreatic tunnel.

The surgeon isolates the common bile duct with a vessel loop, ensuring no aberrant right hepatic arterial anomalies are present. The duct is transected with a linear stapler to prevent bile spillage. The retropancreatic tunnel over the portal vein is isolated using moistened umbilical tape. The splenic vein is isolated and encircled with a vessel loop, followed by the isolation and transection of the gastroepiploic vein.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas from the lateral border of the SMV portal vein, separating the uncinate process from the vessels in a caudate to cephalid direction. Incident venous branches are ligated. The SMV portal vein is reflected medially to the right of the screen, revealing the SMA posteriorly. The inferior pancreatic duodenal artery is identified, dissected, clipped, and ligated with a ligature divider. The superior pancreatic duodenal vein is identified, dissected, and divided with a stapler.

The surgeon lifts the entire pancreas from the retroperitoneum, with the organ only attached by the gastroduodenal artery, splenic artery, and splenic vein. The splenic artery, gastroduodenal artery, and splenic vein are transected sequentially using vascular load staplers, minimizing warm ischemic time. The pancreatic specimen is then extracted completely, and reconstruction begins while the islets are prepared.

The surgeon reinforces the inferior pancreatic duodenal artery with 5-0 Prolene sutures. The jejunum is brought through a defect in the right mesocolon, and an end-to-side hepaticojejunostomy is performed using a running suture technique. A stent is placed in the bile duct before completing the anterior row of the hepaticojejunostomy.
The surgeon performs a Hofmeister-type hand-sewn end-to-side gastrojejunostomy, meticulously stitching two layers to connect the stomach to the jejunum.

The surgeon partially excises the splenic vein stump staple line and manipulates a fourteen gauge angiocath into the portal vein, securing it with the robotic arm. Sterile tubing is connected to the catheter, and islets are infused. The splenic vein stump is closed with IV-O Surgipro, and the vascular clamp is removed. A staple is applied closer to the portal vein for reinforcement.
